cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The ideological, political, and military rivalry between the United States and Soviet Union from 1945 to 1991 — fought through arms races, proxy wars, propaganda, and economic competition rather than direct military conflict between the superpowers.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Two 1945 meetings between Allied leaders that divided postwar Germany into occupation zones, set terms for Soviet entry into the Pacific war, and exposed the growing rift between Western democracies and the USSR over Eastern Europe.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1947 US policy pledging economic and military aid to any nation — beginning with Greece and Turkey — threatened by communist takeover; formalized the strategy of containment.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1948 US program that delivered roughly $13 billion in aid to rebuild Western European economies after WWII, designed to blunt the appeal of communism by restoring prosperity.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: International organization founded in 1945 to preserve peace through collective security; its permanent-member council (US, USSR, UK, France, China) gave each great power a veto, reflecting the new bipolar order.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The post-1945 process by which European colonies in Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean won independence — sometimes peacefully, sometimes through armed struggle — often shaped by Cold War superpower rivalry.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The principle that peoples should choose their own government and political status — championed in the UN Charter and invoked by colonized nations demanding independence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1948 document adopted by the UN General Assembly that proclaimed universal civil, political, economic, and social rights — drafted partly in response to Nazi atrocities and used by later activists against apartheid and authoritarianism.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1945-1953) who authorized the atomic bombings of Japan, announced the doctrine bearing his name pledging aid against communism, and committed US forces to the Korean War.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Soviet dictator until 1953 who consolidated communist regimes across Eastern Europe after WWII, triggered the Berlin Blockade, and set the confrontational posture that defined the early Cold War.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US Secretary of State who in 1947 proposed the European Recovery Program that now bears his name, later awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for rebuilding war-torn Europe.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Indian independence leader whose philosophy of nonviolent civil disobedience — expressed through the Salt March and Homespun Movement — forced Britain to quit India in 1947 and inspired decolonization movements worldwide.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1949 Western military alliance founded by the US, Canada, and Western European states in which an attack on one member is treated as an attack on all — the cornerstone of containment in Europe. (PDF spelling; commonly 'Treaty'.)

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1955 Soviet-led military alliance of Eastern Bloc states formed in response to West Germany's admission to NATO; enforced communist rule through joint invasions such as Czechoslovakia 1968.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Coalition of newly independent states — led by Nehru, Tito, Nasser, and Nkrumah — that refused to side with either superpower bloc during the Cold War, formalized at the 1955 Bandung Conference.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Winston Churchill's 1946 metaphor for the ideological and physical boundary dividing Soviet-controlled Eastern Europe from the democratic West.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Eisenhower's 1961 warning term describing the entrenched alliance between a nation's military establishment and its defense industries, which he argued could distort public policy.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US Cold War strategy, articulated by diplomat George Kennan, of preventing the spread of Soviet influence beyond its existing sphere through economic aid, alliances, and limited military intervention.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Strategy of discouraging enemy aggression by maintaining credible retaliatory forces — especially nuclear — so that an attack would bring unacceptable costs.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: First prime minister of independent India who led the Non-Alignment Movement, pursued state-led industrialization, and positioned India as a leader of the decolonizing world.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Communist leader of Yugoslavia who broke with Stalin in 1948 and steered a non-aligned course between the superpowers while holding together a multi-ethnic federation.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Soviet premier (1953-1964) who denounced Stalin's cult of personality, pursued 'peaceful coexistence' rhetoric, and yet provoked the Berlin and Cuban Missile Crises. (PDF spelling.)

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Pan-Africanist leader who guided the Gold Coast to independence as Ghana in 1957, becoming the first sub-Saharan African nation to break from European colonial rule.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1961-1963) who faced down the Cuban Missile Crisis, approved the failed Bay of Pigs Invasion, and pledged to land Americans on the Moon.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Egyptian president who nationalized the Suez Canal in 1956, championed pan-Arabism and non-alignment, and commissioned the Soviet-built Aswan Dam.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Soviet cosmonaut who in 1961 became the first human to orbit Earth aboard Vostok 1, giving the USSR an early and symbolic lead in the Space Race.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: American astronaut who in July 1969 became the first person to walk on the Moon during Apollo 11, fulfilling Kennedy's pledge and ending the Space Race in US favor.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Cold War belief that if one country fell to communism, neighboring states would topple in sequence — used to justify US intervention in Korea, Vietnam, and Latin America.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The spread of nuclear weapons and weapons-capable technology to additional states during the Cold War, prompting treaties such as the 1968 Non-Proliferation Treaty.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Cold War doctrine holding that any full-scale nuclear attack by one superpower would be met with overwhelming retaliation, ensuring the annihilation of both and therefore deterring first strikes.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Concrete barrier erected by East Germany in 1961 to stop mass defections to the West; became the defining symbol of Cold War division until its fall in November 1989.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Armed conflicts in which the US and USSR backed opposing local factions — in Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Nicaragua, and Afghanistan — rather than fighting each other directly.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1950-1953 war in which UN forces led by the US fought North Korean and Chinese troops, ending in an armistice that preserved the division of the peninsula near the 38th parallel.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The line of latitude chosen in 1945 to divide Korea into Soviet and US occupation zones, which became the fortified border between North and South Korea after the 1953 armistice.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Cold War competition for supremacy in spaceflight, running from the 1957 Sputnik launch through the 1969 Apollo Moon landing, driven as much by prestige as by military reconnaissance needs.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Failed 1961 CIA-backed landing of Cuban exiles aimed at toppling Fidel Castro; its collapse humiliated the Kennedy administration and pushed Cuba closer to the USSR.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Thirteen-day 1962 standoff triggered by US discovery of Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba — widely regarded as the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war, resolved by a secret missile swap.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Long US military intervention (1955-1975) on behalf of South Vietnam against the communist North and Viet Cong, ending in US withdrawal and the reunification of Vietnam under communist rule.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1980s Reagan-administration scandal in which officials secretly sold arms to Iran and diverted the proceeds to fund the Contra insurgency against Nicaragua's leftist Sandinista government.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: North Korean leader who, with Soviet backing, launched the 1950 invasion of the South and established the Kim family dynastic dictatorship that still rules North Korea.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Vietnamese communist leader who declared Vietnam's independence from France in 1945 and led North Vietnam through wars against France and the United States.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Cuban revolutionary who overthrew Batista in 1959, nationalized US businesses, allied with the USSR, and ruled Cuba as a one-party communist state for nearly half a century.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Argentine-born Marxist revolutionary who helped Castro seize power in Cuba and later died attempting to export guerrilla revolution to Bolivia, becoming a global leftist icon.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1963-1969) who massively escalated American ground forces in Vietnam after the Gulf of Tonkin incident while pushing through the Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1969-1974) who opened diplomatic relations with communist China, pursued détente with the USSR through SALT I, and withdrew US troops from Vietnam before resigning over Watergate.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1974-1977) who took office after Nixon's resignation, oversaw the final fall of Saigon in 1975, and signed the Helsinki Accords recognizing postwar European borders.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Soviet leader (1964-1982) whose doctrine justified invading Warsaw Pact states that strayed from communism; his era saw SALT-era détente followed by the invasion of Afghanistan.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Political party founded in 1921 that, under Mao Zedong, won the Chinese Civil War in 1949 and has ruled the People's Republic of China as a single-party state ever since.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1934-1935 6,000-mile retreat of Mao's Red Army from Nationalist encirclement in southern China to a remote northern base, transforming Mao into the CCP's paramount leader.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1919 Chinese student-led protest against the Treaty of Versailles' transfer of German holdings in Shandong to Japan; sparked new waves of nationalism and Marxist organizing.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Struggle between the Nationalist Kuomintang under Chiang Kai-shek and the Communists under Mao Zedong — fought from 1927 with a WWII pause — won by the CCP in 1949.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Early CCP policy after 1949 that seized fields from landlords and reassigned them to peasants, often through public trials and executions, cementing rural support for the regime.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Mao's 1958-1962 campaign to industrialize China through backyard steel furnaces and collective farms; produced the deadliest famine in history, killing tens of millions.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Mao's 1957-1959 purge that followed the Hundred Flowers Campaign, persecuting intellectuals, party critics, and perceived class enemies through labor camps and public denunciations.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Militant youth paramilitary groups mobilized by Mao in 1966 to attack teachers, officials, and cultural heritage during the Cultural Revolution.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Mao's 1966-1976 campaign to purge capitalist and traditional elements from Chinese society, closing schools, persecuting intellectuals, and producing widespread chaos and violence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Series of modernizing reforms launched by Iran's Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi in 1963 — land reform, women's suffrage, literacy drives — that alienated clergy and set the stage for the 1979 revolution.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1952 Guatemalan agrarian-reform law under President Árbenz that expropriated unused United Fruit Company land, triggering a CIA-backed coup in 1954.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1983-1985 mass starvation intensified by the Mengistu regime's war policies and drought, which killed hundreds of thousands and drew unprecedented global humanitarian response.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1985 transcontinental benefit rock concert organized by Bob Geldof to raise funds for Ethiopian famine relief, watched by an estimated 1.9 billion viewers worldwide.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Founding father of the Republic of China and the Kuomintang, whose 'Three Principles of the People' (nationalism, democracy, livelihood) shaped early twentieth-century Chinese politics.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Nationalist Kuomintang leader who unified China in the late 1920s, fought Japan and the Communists, and after losing the Civil War ruled Taiwan until his death in 1975.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Iran's last shah, installed after a 1953 CIA-backed coup against Mosaddegh; his Westernizing White Revolution and SAVAK secret police fueled the 1979 Islamic Revolution.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Shia cleric who led the 1979 Iranian Revolution that toppled the Shah and founded the Islamic Republic of Iran, which he ruled as Supreme Leader until 1989.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Ethiopian military officer who led the 1974 Derg coup against Emperor Haile Selassie and ruled as a Soviet-aligned Marxist dictator through famine and civil war until 1991.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Nonviolent refusal to obey unjust laws — theorized by Thoreau, practiced by Gandhi in the Salt March, and later adopted by Martin Luther King Jr. in the US civil-rights movement.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Gandhi-led campaign urging Indians to boycott British textiles and instead spin and wear their own cotton cloth (khadi), linking economic self-reliance to independence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Gandhi's 1930 240-mile walk to the Arabian Sea in defiance of the British salt monopoly — the signature act of mass civil disobedience that galvanized Indian independence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Political party founded in 1906 under Jinnah that advocated a separate Muslim homeland, leading to the 1947 partition of British India into India and Pakistan.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: First sub-Saharan African colony to gain independence (1957), led by Kwame Nkrumah out of the British Gold Coast and held up as a model for pan-African liberation.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1961-1975 armed struggle against Portuguese colonial rule that transitioned directly into a Cold War proxy civil war among MPLA, FNLA, and UNITA factions.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Breakaway southeastern Nigerian republic declared in 1967 by the Igbo; its failed secession sparked the Nigerian Civil War and a famine that killed over a million.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Kikuyu-led 1952-1960 insurgency against British settler rule in Kenya that accelerated decolonization despite harsh British counterinsurgency and detention camps.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: South Vietnamese communist guerrilla force supported by North Vietnam that fought US and South Vietnamese troops during the Vietnam War, notably during the 1968 Tet Offensive.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Ideology promoting the political and cultural unity of African peoples across the continent and diaspora — championed by W.E.B. Du Bois, Nkrumah, and the Organization of African Unity.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: French-Canadian nationalist movement that from the 1960s Quiet Revolution pressed for political, linguistic, and cultural autonomy — including unsuccessful referendums on Quebec sovereignty.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Leader of the All-India Muslim League and founding governor-general of Pakistan in 1947, whose push for a separate Muslim state produced the partition of British India.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Prime minister of India (1966-1977, 1980-1984) who led India to victory in the 1971 war that created Bangladesh, imposed a controversial Emergency, and was assassinated by Sikh bodyguards.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: South Africa's 1948-1994 state system of legally enforced racial segregation and white-minority rule, dismantled after decades of resistance led by the African National Congress.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: South African political party founded in 1912 that led the anti-apartheid struggle — including armed resistance under Mandela's Umkhonto we Sizwe — and has governed since 1994.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1970s-1980s covert campaign by right-wing South American dictatorships — with US backing — to hunt down and assassinate leftist dissidents across borders.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1936-1939 conflict in which Franco's Nationalists, aided by Hitler and Mussolini, defeated the Republican government, establishing a fascist dictatorship that lasted until 1975.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Nickname for Idi Amin, whose 1971-1979 military dictatorship in Uganda carried out mass killings, expelled the Asian population, and caused an estimated 300,000 deaths.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: The use of violence or intimidation against civilians to achieve political aims — a defining tactic of late-twentieth-century groups such as the IRA, Shining Path, and Al Qaeda.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Paramilitary organization that fought for the reunification of Northern Ireland with the Republic of Ireland, waging a bombing and shooting campaign against British rule through the 1990s.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: January 1972 incident in Derry, Northern Ireland, when British paratroopers killed 14 unarmed Catholic civil-rights marchers, intensifying the Troubles.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Maoist guerrilla insurgency in Peru led by Abimael Guzmán from 1980 whose campaign of bombings and village massacres killed tens of thousands before his 1992 capture.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Transnational Sunni Islamist terrorist network founded by Osama bin Laden in the late 1980s, responsible for the 1998 embassy bombings and the September 11 attacks.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Coordinated Al Qaeda hijackings that destroyed the World Trade Center and struck the Pentagon, killing nearly 3,000 people and launching the US-led War on Terror.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: ANC leader imprisoned 27 years for resisting apartheid who, upon release, negotiated South Africa's democratic transition and became its first Black president in 1994.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Last apartheid-era president of South Africa who legalized the ANC, released Mandela in 1990, and shared the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize for ending white-minority rule.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Chilean general who seized power in a 1973 CIA-backed coup against Salvador Allende and ruled as dictator until 1990, overseeing mass killings, disappearances, and neoliberal reforms.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Spanish general who won the Spanish Civil War in 1939 and ruled Spain as a fascist-aligned authoritarian dictator until his death in 1975.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Ugandan military dictator (1971-1979) whose brutal regime, expulsion of Asians, and mass killings earned him the nickname 'Butcher of Uganda' before he was ousted by Tanzania.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Saudi-born founder of Al Qaeda who orchestrated the September 11, 2001 attacks and evaded capture for nearly a decade before US Navy SEALs killed him in Pakistan in 2011.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Massive hydroelectric dam on the Nile commissioned by Nasser and built with Soviet aid in the 1960s after the US withdrew funding over Cold War disputes.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: October 1973 surprise attack on Israel by Egypt and Syria during the Jewish high holy day; its near-defeat of Israel triggered US nuclear alerts and the 1973 Arab oil embargo.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Concrete and fence wall begun by Israel in 2002 separating the West Bank from Israeli territory, framed by Israel as an anti-terror measure and by Palestinians as a land grab. (PDF spelling preserved.)

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1972 Munich Summer Games attack in which the Palestinian group Black September took Israeli athletes hostage; 11 Israelis and a German officer died in the failed rescue.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1947 division of British India into Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan that triggered communal violence, ~14 million refugees, and up to a million deaths.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1967 policy statement by Tanzania's Julius Nyerere committing the country to Ujamaa — an African socialism built on self-reliance, collective villages, and nationalization.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Julius Nyerere's Ujamaa program in Tanzania that resettled peasants into collective villages, nationalized banks and plantations, and prioritized literacy and rural development.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Chinese-financed railway completed in 1975 linking Zambia's Copperbelt to Tanzania's coast, freeing landlocked Zambia from dependence on apartheid South Africa and Rhodesia.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Term for the European imperial 'mother country' at the center of a colonial network (e.g., Britain for the Raj, France for Algeria), to which colonies remained economically tied even after independence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Maoist guerrilla movement led by Pol Pot that seized Cambodia in 1975, emptied cities into agrarian labor camps, and killed roughly 1.7 million people before Vietnamese forces ousted it in 1979.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Mass graves and execution sites across Cambodia where the Khmer Rouge buried victims of its 1975-1979 genocide, memorialized today at sites such as Choeung Ek.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Prime minister of Israel (1969-1974) who led the country through the Munich Olympics crisis and the 1973 Yom Kippur War, earning her nickname as Israel's 'Iron Lady.'

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Cambodian king-turned-politician who navigated Cold War pressures, was deposed in 1970, briefly allied with the Khmer Rouge, and returned as monarch after the UN-brokered settlement.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Tanzania's founding president whose Arusha Declaration launched Ujamaa African socialism while promoting pan-African unity and supporting liberation movements in southern Africa.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Sri Lankan leader who in 1960 became the world's first female prime minister, served three terms, and pursued non-alignment and nationalization policies.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1970s easing of US-Soviet tensions marked by Nixon-Brezhnev summits, the SALT arms-limitation treaties, and expanded trade and cultural exchange — reversed in the late 1970s.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1979-1989 Soviet intervention on behalf of Afghanistan's communist government against US- and Pakistan-backed mujahideen — often called the 'Soviet Vietnam' for its cost and failure.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Strategic Arms Limitation Treaties (1972, 1979) that capped US and Soviet nuclear delivery systems; SALT II was never ratified by the US Senate after the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Upset 1980 Winter Olympics hockey victory of the amateur US team over the heavily favored Soviet squad at Lake Placid — celebrated in the US as a Cold War symbolic triumph.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Moscow Games boycotted by the United States and dozens of allied nations in protest of the 1979 Soviet invasion of Afghanistan.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Los Angeles Games boycotted in retaliation by the Soviet Union and most Eastern Bloc countries, keeping Cold War politics on the Olympic stage.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Nickname for Reagan's 1983 Strategic Defense Initiative proposing a space-based missile shield against Soviet ICBMs — technically unproven but strategically pressured Moscow.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: 1986 Soviet nuclear plant explosion in Ukraine that released massive radioactive fallout across Europe and helped discredit the USSR's claims of technological and political competence.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Gorbachev's 1980s 'restructuring' reforms allowing limited private enterprise, decentralized planning, and managerial autonomy in a bid to revive the stagnant Soviet economy.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Gorbachev's 'openness' policy that relaxed censorship, permitted public criticism of officials, and exposed Soviet failings — unleashing forces that contributed to the USSR's collapse.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Chinese population-control program (1979-2015) limiting most urban couples to a single child; reduced births by hundreds of millions but produced a gender imbalance and aging crisis.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: June 1989 crackdown in which Chinese troops killed hundreds to thousands of pro-democracy student protesters in Beijing, ending the reform movement and hardening one-party rule.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: US president (1981-1989) whose arms buildup, 'Evil Empire' rhetoric, and Star Wars initiative pressured the USSR, while his summits with Gorbachev produced the INF Treaty.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Final Soviet leader (1985-1991) whose glasnost and perestroika reforms, withdrawal from Afghanistan, and refusal to use force in Eastern Europe enabled the end of the Cold War.

cards.subject: ap-world-history
cards.prompt: Chinese paramount leader after Mao who launched 'Reform and Opening Up' in 1978 — introducing market reforms and Special Economic Zones — while crushing the 1989 Tiananmen protests.
